era_context: The Second Punic War (218–201 BCE) dominates Mediterranean geopolitics; Hannibal Barca commands Carthaginian forces in Iberia and Italy after crossing the Alps in 218 BCE. Rome is under severe military pressure but maintains control of the sea and central Italy. In the East, the Seleucid Empire under Antiochus III is expanding; Egypt under Ptolemy IV faces internal instability. China is in the late Warring States period, approaching unification under Qin.

events.title: Battle of the Trebia River (or aftermath consolidation)
events.description: Hannibal's forces, having defeated the Romans at Trebia in 218 BCE, consolidate control over Cisalpine Gaul and recruit Gallic allies. By 215 BCE, Hannibal is preparing for deeper penetration into Italy. This year marks the continuation of Carthaginian military dominance in the peninsula and the recruitment of additional Celtic and Iberian forces to sustain the campaign.
key_figures: Publius Cornelius Scipio (Roman commander, recovering from Trebia wounds)

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (Ab Urbe Condita), Books 21–30

events.certainty_note: The broad outline of Hannibal's campaign is well-attested, but precise year-by-year movements are reconstructed from later sources written 1–2 centuries after events. Exact dating of intermediate campaigns is uncertain.

events.title: Treaty of Alliance between Hannibal and Philip V of Macedon
events.description: Hannibal, seeking to open a second front against Rome, negotiates an alliance with Philip V of Macedon. This treaty, often dated to 215 BCE or shortly thereafter, commits Philip to attack Roman interests in the Balkans and Adriatic while Hannibal presses from the west. The alliance reflects Hannibal's strategic vision of encircling Rome but ultimately fails to coordinate effectively, as Philip's campaigns are limited by Roman naval superiority and the intervention of the Aetolian League.
events.certainty_note: The treaty is attested in multiple ancient sources, but the exact date (215 or 214 BCE) is debated by modern scholars. Polybius and Livy provide the main accounts, written well after the events.

events.title: First Macedonian War begins
events.description: Following the treaty with Hannibal, Philip V launches military operations against Roman allies in the Balkans and Adriatic. Roman forces under Laevinus respond, initiating the First Macedonian War (215–205 BCE). This conflict, though secondary to the main Punic War, ties down Roman resources and demonstrates Rome's ability to fight on multiple fronts simultaneously. The war ends inconclusively with the Peace of Phoenice in 205 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The First Macedonian War is well-documented in ancient sources and its dates are securely established by modern scholarship.

events.title: Antiochus III campaigns in Coele-Syria
events.description: Antiochus III of the Seleucid Empire, known as 'the Great,' continues his expansionist campaigns in the Levant, targeting Coele-Syria (the region between the Lebanon and Anti-Lebanon mountains) and Phoenicia. These campaigns are part of his broader effort to restore Seleucid control over territories lost to the Ptolemaic Empire. The Fourth Syrian War (219–217 BCE) is ongoing, with Antiochus initially successful but ultimately defeated by Ptolemy IV at Raphia in 217 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The broad outline of Antiochus III's campaigns is well-attested, but precise year-by-year chronology is reconstructed from later sources. The Fourth Syrian War dates are established by modern scholarship.

events.title: Ptolemy IV consolidates Egyptian control after internal instability
events.description: Ptolemy IV Philopator, who ascended to the throne around 221 BCE, faces significant internal challenges including court intrigue and the influence of powerful ministers. By 215 BCE, he is consolidating his authority in preparation for the Fourth Syrian War against Antiochus III. His reign is marked by cultural patronage and religious policy, including the promotion of the cult of Serapis and the integration of Egyptian religious traditions with Hellenistic practices.
events.certainty_note: Ptolemaic chronology is well-established, but details of internal politics are reconstructed from fragmentary sources including papyri and later historical accounts.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Hannibal's treaty with Philip V is disputed; some scholars place it in 214 BCE rather than 215 BCE. Additionally, the significance of the First Macedonian War is sometimes overstated in Western historiography—it was militarily inconclusive and did not substantially affect the outcome of the Second Punic War. Modern scholarship emphasizes that Rome's naval superiority prevented Philip from effectively supporting Hannibal.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 215 BCE is rich but heavily dependent on Livy and Polybius, both writing well after the events (Polybius in the 2nd century BCE, Livy in the 1st century BCE). These sources are generally reliable for major political and military events but may contain errors in precise dating and tactical details. The focus is heavily on Mediterranean affairs; events in Asia, Africa beyond Egypt, and the Americas are virtually absent from surviving sources. Chinese sources for this period are independent and detailed but do not overlap with Mediterranean events.

graph_edges.note: Hannibal's consolidation in 215 BCE leads to the major offensive culminating in the Battle of Cannae in 216 BCE
The treaty with Philip V directly triggers the First Macedonian War
Antiochus III's campaigns in Coele-Syria lead to the Fourth Syrian War and the Battle of Raphia
